assay_name: Absorbance measurement
assay_description: measurement of absorbance intensity of chemical compounds

assay_name: Growth inhibition of Klebsiella pneumonia
assay_description: Klebsiella pneumonia, DSM681 = ATCC 10031, was cultivated in MHB in 384 well plates. 250nL of 10 mM solutions of each compound in DMSO were transferred with the acoustic dispenser Echo 550 to each well. The cultivation volume was 60 µL, plates were incubated at 37°C for 24h. The optical density at 600nm was determined and used as measure for bacterial growth. Data from control wells with medium only were used as 100% inhibition, and data from control wells with bacteria only were set as 0% growth inhibition. 50% growth inhibition was chosen as threshold to identify active compounds.

assay_name: Inhibition of Firefly Luciferase activity
assay_description:
To identify compounds interfering with Firefly luciferase in ECBL screening library an enzymatic assay was applied.  The compound plates (384 LDV Labcyte # LP-0200)  were thawed and centrifuged prior to transferring of 20  nL of 10 mM stocks to the assay plates (LUMITRAC 384 white, Greiner #781075) using Echo 650T (Labcyte) acoustic liquid handler. DMSO (VWR # 83673.290) and  PTC-124 {3-[5-(2-fluorophenyl)-1,2,4-oxadiazol-3-yl]benzoic acid} (VWR # BIOV9421-10) were added as negative and positive controls to the columns 23 and 24 of each assay plate to yield 0.1% and 1 µM final concentration respectively. Multidrop Combi (ThermoFisher) non-contact dispenser was used to add 10 µL solution of 20 nM Firefly luciferase enzyme (Promega # E1701) in assay buffer : 0.05 M glycine (Bioshop Canada # GLN001), 0.5 mM EDTA (ThermoFisherScientific cat. no. AM9260G), 5 mM magnesium sulfate (Sigma cat. no. 746452), adjusted to pH 7.6 with Trizma (Sigma cat. no. T6066) and containing 0,005 % sodium azide (Sigma cat. no. 71289) per well and compounds were pre-incubated with the enzyme for 10 min at RT.  Then, the enzymatic reaction was initiated by adding of 10 µL beetle luciferin ( Promega cat. no. E1603) solution in assay buffer containing 20 µM ATP (Bioshop Canada cat. no. ATP007) and 0,2% BSA (MP Biomedicals # 160069) to final concentration of 10 µM (final concentrations of Firefly :10 nM, ATP: 10 µM, BSA: 0.1% ) with Multidrop Combi.  After 10 min luminescent signal was measured with the integration time of 0,2 sec using ClarioStar (BMG Labtech) plate reader.
KNIME pipeline was used for analysis of the results. The raw data files (one txt file per plate) were read and matched with plate template followed by calculation of averages and standard deviation of controls and compound wells, and Z’ factor values for each plate. The plates were proceeded in the loop, generating one data set for all tested plates. Automatic outliers removal was used for detecting control outliers for plates with Z’ factor values below 0,5.  The plates for which removal of the outliers did not lead to improvement of Z’ factor (to value higher than 0,5) or the algorithm was detecting more than 2 outliers, were retested. 
The raw data values were normalised to positive and negative controls for each plate, to calculate percent inhibition following the formula: % INHIBITION = (1-(x- μp)/ μn – μp))*100, where X represents Relative Luminescence Units (RLU) detected for the well,  μn the mean of the negative controls and μp the mean of the positive controls for each tested assay plate. To identify active compounds the thresholds of 30% was applied. Compounds showing %INHIBITION above 30% and below -30% were consider as hits- inhibitors and activators respectively. All the primary hits were then re-tested in duplicates under the same assay conditions. Compounds for which the activity was confirmed for at least one replicate (above 30 or below -30 %INHIBITION) are flagged as ACTIVE.

assay_name: Inhibition of Renilla Luciferase activity
assay_description:
Renilla luciferase activity assay was performed on previous selected compounds (509 compounds) from ECBL screening library in 8 concentrations (concentration range: 0.0125 – 50 µM) in duplicates (duplicate plates). All compounds were in 10 mM stocks on LDV 384-well plate (384 LDV Labcyte # LP-0200). A 100-fold diluted compound plates were prepared by transfer 80 nL using Echo 650T (Labcyte) acoustic liquid handler to 384-well plates (384 LDV Labcyte # LP-0200) and diluted by dispense 8 µL DMSO (VWR # 83673.290) using a non-contact dispenser Multidrop Combi (ThermoFisher). Source plates and prepared plates with 100 times diluted stock solutions were thawed and centrifuged prior to transferring of 100; 32.5; 10 nL and 2.5 from 10 mM stocks and same amount from 0.1 mM stocks to the assay plates (LUMITRAC 384 white, Greiner #781075) using Echo 650T (Labcyte) acoustic liquid handler. DMSO was also transferred by ECHO to a volume of 100 nL. DMSO (VWR # 83673.290) and BTS (VWR cat. no. J64910.X0) were added as negative and positive controls to the columns 23 and 24 of each assay plate to yield 0.5% and 10 µM final concentration respectively. Multidrop Combi (ThermoFisher) non-contact dispenser was used to add 10 µL solution of 100 pM Renilla enzyme (RayBiotech cat. no. RB-15-0003P-50) in enzyme buffer: 50 mM potassium phosphate (Sigma cat. no. P5379), 500 mM NaCl, 1 mM EDTA (Sigma cat. no. E5134), pH 7.4 set with NaOH (POCH cat. no. 810981424) per well and compounds were pre-incubated with the enzyme for 10 min at RT.  Then, the enzymatic reaction was initiated by adding of 10 µL coelentrazine   solution in	substrate buffer: 0.1 M Tris-HCl pH 7.4/0.3 M Sodium ascorbate to final concentration of 2 µM (final concentrations of Renilla : 50 pM) with Multidrop Combi.  After 10 min luminescent signal was measured with the integration time of 0,2 sec using ClarioStar (BMG Labtech) plate reader. 
KNIME pipeline was generated and applied for analysis of the results. The raw data files (one txt file per pate) were read and matched with plate template followed by calculation of averages and standard deviation of controls and compound wells, and Z’ factor values for each plate. The plates were proceeded in the loop, generating one data set for all tested plates. Automatic outlier removal was used for detecting control outliers for plates with Z’ factor values below 0,5. The plates for which removal of the outliers did not lead to improvement of Z’ factor (higher then 0,5) or the algorithm was detecting more than 2 outliers, were retested. 
The raw data values were normalized to positive and negative control for each plate, to calculate percent inhibition following the formula: % INHIBITION = (1-(x- μp)/ (μn – μp))* 100, where X represents readout VALUE of the well,  μp the mean of the positive controls and μn represents the mean of the negative controls for each tested assay plate. The heat map of % INHIBITION was then generated for each plate in order to identify plate effects or signal distribution patterns. Plates with visible patterns or abnormal distribution were retested.
From the data table, the compound's concentration, % inhibition, and compound ID were selected and using a pipeline in KNIME and a node that utilizes R programming, dose-response curves were generated for each compound. All curves were generated using LL.4 regression model. The IC50 values were determined from the inflection points of the generated curves. 
In relation to the conducted assay aimed to identify compounds which may cause interferences with Renilla luciferase (commonly use in biological and biochemical assays), the following criterion was adopted. All compounds were marked as ACTIVE when they exhibited activity above 30% in two concentrations and two replicates. Other additional information was placed in the comments in results file.

assay_name: Academic compound collection, Validation screening (EUACC Batch 04) cell viability ATP quantification assay with HepG2 cells
assay_description:
Validation screen with 52 compounds that showed at least 30% Inhibition in the primary screen. 
Plate type: 384-well plate Corning 3764 Black clear bottom.
750 cells /well seeded into the plate. 
Cell culture medium: DMEM (1.0 g/L glucose, without L-Glutamine, Lonza 12-707F), 10% FBS (Gibco 10270106), 2 mM L-glutamine (Lonza BE17-605E), 2% Pen/Strep (Lonza DE17-602E), + 1% NEAA (Gibco 11140-035)
 48 hour incubation time.
Cell viability ATP quantification assay with HepG2 cells. Assay kit: CellTiter-Glo®️ 2.0 Cell Viability Assay - Promega Corporation. 
15uL CellTiter-Glo/well added.
Compounds were tested with 8 doses in triplicates in independent experiments. Screening concentrations were 0.01, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 50 uM. Luminescence was detected using Pherastar FS reader. Data was normalized to the low (10 uM Sepantronium bromide - YM155) and high controls (25 nL/well Dimethyl Sulfoxide - DMSO) to generate percent inhibition values. The percent inhibition values uploaded. 
 Curve fitting was done using four parameter hill fitting algorithm. AUC, IC50, low and high asymptote values are reported.

assay_name: Dose-dependent inhibition of SARS-CoV-2 cytopathic effect in VeroE6
assay_description:
To measure inhibition of the SARS-CoV-2 cytopathic effect, 384-well imaging plates (Greiner 781092) were spotted with test compounds and controls (16 positive and 16 negative control wells per plate)  using an acoustic dispenser (Echo, Labcyte) to yield 8-points dilution series of final test compound concentration of 10 nM - 33 µM  at 0.1% vol/vol  DMSO in triplicates . One day before infection (Day -1), test plates were equilibrated to room temperature and 30 µL of Vero E6 EGFP cells were added at 8,000 cells/well. On the day of infection (Day 0), plates were transported to the CAPS-IT robotic system for the addition of virus (MOI = 0.001) using a liquid handler (EVO 100, Tecan) to a final volume of 60 µl, and incubated at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 4 days. Plates were then imaged on an Arrayscan XTI, (Thermofisher)
At day four post-infection, the GFP signal was captured using wide field fluorescence imaging by exciting at 485-20 nm and emitting with the BGRFRN filter set. A 5 X objective captured 80% of the well bottom area in the 384 plate. The optimal exposure time was determined based on fluorescence intensity and was set as 0.023 seconds. A 2x2 binning was used and autofocus plane count was reduced to increase image acquisition speed. An image analysis protocol was developed in-house by using the SpotDetector bioapplication (Cellomics, Thermofisher). After background reduction on the raw image files, a fixed fluorescent intensity threshold was determined for the identification of fluorescent cells. Afterwards, the total area (‘total amount of surface covered by fluorescent cells’) was calculated for the test compounds and control wells.
Test well results are normalized relative to the corresponding +/- Remdesivir controls giving % confluence as primary endpoint. Control well outliers are eliminated according to 3-sigma method and plate level statistical performance is assessed using the standard Z’ calculation. 
Dose-Response curve fits are performed using KNIME HCS tools (https://github.com/knime-mpicbg/HCS-Tools/wiki). The workflow is published on GitHUB (https://github.com/Fraunhofer-ITMP/KNIME/tree/main/Dose_response_workflow) and KNIME hub (https://hub.knime.com/fraunhoferitmp/spaces/Public/latest/Dose_Response_Cell-based-Assay/DRC_template_WithOutlierDetection~SwmoxbJewJ1k8Dcd). In brief, all triplicate data-points exceeding 25% standard-deviation are eliminated from curve-fit and were flagged as outliers. All concentration means showing a reduction in confluence of more the 10% confluence compared to the 0.5-log lower compound concentration are classified as toxic concentrations and are eliminated from the curve fit. Remaining datapoints are fitted using a four parameter logistic fit.

assay_name: C. auris DSM21092 Anti-Fungal Assay
assay_description:
Screening of the 3 rd batch of the Academic Library  (1408 compounds) for 1 concentration (50 μM) with 2 replicates against C. auris DSM21092, according to “N8: Anti-Bacterial and Anti-Fungal Assays Bioprofiling Handbook” and following the protocols optimized during the validation step indicated in that proposal (Under the frame of the EU-OS bioprofiling tender process), to determine the antifungal properties of these compounds against this human pathogen.
Briefly, compounds were tested in a liquid growth medium dispensed in 384 well plates inoculated with a standardized fungal suspension and after overnight incubation at 37ºC the plates were examined for fungal growth as absorbance readout. The Genedata Screener software (Genedata, Inc., Basel, Switzerland) was used to process and analyze all the data from the screen. The reproducibility and sensitivity were supported by the statistical values derived from all the experiments performed. The activity of the compounds was expressed as a percentage of growth inhibition where 100% represented the inhibition growth of the target microorganism and 0% represented the total growth of target microorganism on the assay.

assay_name: E. faecalis ATCC 29212 Anti-Bacterial Assay
assay_description:
Screening of 46 compounds from the EACL Academic Compound library (Batch1+Batch2) for Hit Confirmation in Dose Response Curves with 3 replicates against E. faecalis ATCC 29212, according to “N8: Anti-Bacterial and Anti-Fungal Assays Bioprofiling Handbook” and following the protocols optimized during the validation step indicated in that proposal (Under the frame of the EU-OS bioprofiling tender process), to determine the antibacterial properties of these compounds against this human pathogen.
Briefly, compounds were tested in a liquid growth medium dispensed in 384 well plates inoculated with a standardized bacterial suspension (CFU/mL) and after overnight incubation at 37ºC the plates were examined for bacterial growth as absorbance readout. Each compound was serially diluted in DMSO to provide 7 different final assay concentrations: 50 µM, 10 µM, 3 µM, 1 µM, 0.3 µM, 0.1 µM and 0.01 µM. The Genedata Screener software (Genedata, Inc., Basel, Switzerland) was used to process and analyze all the data from the screen. The reproducibility and sensitivity were supported by the statistical values derived from all the experiments performed. The activity of the compounds was expressed as a percentage of growth inhibition where 100% represented the inhibition growth of the target microorganism and 0% represented the total growth of target microorganism on the assay.

assay_name: Dose-dependent inhibition of SARS-CoV-2 3CL-Pro at 1mM DTT
assay_description:
The activity of 3-CLpro (GenBank : MN908947.3 Residues 3264-3569) was measured by a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), using the dual-labeled substrate, DABCYL-KTSAVLQ↓SGFRKM-EDANS (Bachem #4045664) containing a protease specific cleavage site after the Gln. In the intact peptide, EDANS fluorescence is quenched by the DABCYL group. Following enzymatic cleavage, generation of the fluorescent product was monitored (Ex/Em = 340/460 nm) (EnVision, PerkinElmer). In the alternate profiling assay, DTT insensitive hits identified in the hit profiling screen were profiled in the presence of 1 mM DTT. Test compounds (11-concentrations, 2-fold dilution series, top at 10 mM stock concentration in 100 % DMSO), positive (zinc pyrithione (medchemexpress, #HY-B0572) 10 mM in 100 % DMSO) and negative (100 % DMSO) controls, were transferred to 384-well assay microplates by acoustic dispensing (Echo, Labcyte). Plate locations were: test compounds at 0.02 µM - 20 μM final (columns 1 to 22); positive control zinc pyrithione at 10 μM final (column 23); and negative control 0.2 % v/v (column 24). 5 μl of SARS-CoV-2 3CL-Pro stock (120 nM) in assay buffer (20 mM Tris (pH 7.3), 100 mM NaCl, and 1mM EDTA, 1mM DTT) were added to compound plates and incubated for 60 min at 37 °C. After addition of 5 μl substrate (30 μM in assay buffer), the final concentrations were: 15 μM substrate; 60 nM SARS-CoV-2 3CL-Pro; 0.02 µM - 20 μM compound; and 0.2 % DMSO in a total volume of 10 μL/well. The fluorescence signal was then measured at 15 min and inhibition (%) calculated relative to controls. Results were normalized to the 100 % (positive control) and 0 % (negative control) inhibition. 
Outliers are eliminated according to 3-sigma method and plate level statistical performance is assessed using the standard Z’ calculation.
Dose-Response curve fits are performed using KNIME HCS tools (https://github.com/knime-mpicbg/HCS-Tools/wiki). TThe workflow is published on GitHUB (https://github.com/Fraunhofer-ITMP/KNIME/tree/main/Dose_response_workflow_biochemical) and KNIME hub (https://hub.knime.com/fraunhoferitmp/spaces/Public/latest/Dose_Response_Biochemical/DRCfit_biochemical_ECBD~6NLZB5Jkgn6j5a6Y).  In brief,  all data is fitted accordign to a four-parameter free fit model. IC50 values were not extrapolated. Compounds were classified as active when: 
- maximum (average % inhibion) >25 % and slope is >0 and IC50 > 5 µM or
- minimum (average % inhibion) >75 %

assay_name: Dose Response Inhibition of ARC4/tankyrase binding motif interaction
assay_description:
The assay is based on the interaction of the human TNKS2 protein binding domain ARC4 with a tankyrase binding motif consisting of 8 amino acids. The ARC4 domain is produced as fusion protein with cyan fluorescent protein (CFP, variant mCerulean), while the tankyrase binding motif is produced as fusion protein with yellow fluorescent protein (YFP, variant mCitrine). Proteins are recombinantly produced in E. coli in Oulu.
Upon interaction, the CFP is brought into proximity of the YFP, resulting in a Förster-resonance-energy transfer (FRET) from excitation of CFP at 430 nm resulting in emission from YFP at 527 nm.  We therefore measure the fluorescence intensity of the emission of CFP (477 nm, donor emission) and YFP (527 nm, acceptor emission) upon CFP excitation at 430 nm. We calculate the ratiometric FRET signal (YFP emission:CFP emission, r430) after fluorescence readout and blank subtraction for each well.
Compounds are tested at eight concentrations in duplicate, ranging from 0.5 to 100 uM.

Briefly, compounds were tested in a liquid growth medium dispensed in 384 well plates inoculated with a standardized fungal suspension (CFU/mL) and after overnight incubation at 37ºC the plates were examined for bacterial growth as absorbance readout. Each compound was serially diluted in DMSO to provide 7 different final assay concentrations: 50 µM, 10 µM, 3 µM, 1 µM, 0.3 µM, 0.1 µM and 0.01 µM. The Genedata Screener software (Genedata, Inc., Basel, Switzerland) was used to process and analyze all the data from the screen. The reproducibility and sensitivity were supported by the statistical values derived from all the experiments performed. The activity of the compounds was expressed as a percentage of growth inhibition where 100% represented the inhibition growth of the target microorganism and 0% represented the total growth of target microorganism on the assay.

assay_name: BRET Assay for Mitochondrial ER Contact sites modulators
assay_description: A stable cell line containing a Bioluminescence Resonance Energy Transfer (BRET)-based biosensor for the quantitative analysis of distances between ER that allows to study contact site reversibility and dynamics. This BRET-based biosensor, consists in a mitochondrial outer membrane (MOM)-targeted Rluc8 (acting as donor) and ER-targeted mVenus (as acceptor), that allows studying the distance between mitochondria and the ER membrane with minimal interference.

assay_name: Inhibition of Escherichia coli - academic 1st dose response
assay_description: The growth inhibitory potential of compounds for E.coli ATCC 25922 is detected via the turbidity of the solutions at 600 nm (OD600). Compounds showing a growth inhibition > 50% in the primary assay were chosen for dose response studies and IC50-calculation. Compounds which showed a clear dose dependent effect were labeled as active independent on the IC50-value.

assay_name: Growth inhibition of Klebsiella pneumonia - academic 1st dose response
assay_description: Klebsiella pneumonia, DSM681 = ATCC 10031, was cultivated in MHB in 384 well plates. 250nL of 10 mM solutions of each compound in DMSO were transferred with the acoustic dispenser Echo 550 to each well. The cultivation volume was 60 µL, plates were incubated at 37°C for 24h. The optical density at 600nm was determined and used as measure for bacterial growth. Data from control wells with medium only were used as 100% inhibition, and data from control wells with bacteria only were set as 0% growth inhibition. 50% growth inhibition was chosen as threshold to identify active compounds, of which the dose-response was studied.

assay_name: DSF stabilization of ARC4
assay_description: The assay is based on the melting temperature of the human TNKS2 protein binding domain ARC4 as measured using differential scanning fluorimetry. Hit compounds that inhibited ARC4-TBM-peptide interaction from the screening campaign were tested at 100 uM with 5 μM TNKS2 ARC4 in buffer with SYPRO™ Orange dye. Interaction of ARC4 with a tankyrase binding motif peptide (REAGDGEE peptide, 100 μM) results in stabilization of ARC4 observable as an increase in melting temperature compared to ARC4 alone, and this serves as a positive control. Useful inhibitors of ARC4 function should also increase the melting temperature of ARC4 compared to ARC4 with compound vehicle (DMSO, negative control). The temperature was gradually raised from 20°C to 95°C (1°C increase per minute) with fluorescence measurements performed at intervals of 1 minute. After the experiment, data were normalized and analysed with GraphPad Prism 10 by using a nonlinear regression analysis (Boltzmann sigmoid equation).

assay_name: Single dose inhibition of SARS-CoV-2 cytopathic effect in VeroE6
assay_description:
To measure inhibition of the SARS-CoV-2 cytopathic effect, 384-well imaging plates (Greiner 781092) were spotted with test compounds and controls (16 positive and 16 negative control wells per plate)  using an acoustic dispenser (Echo, Labcyte) to yield 10 µM final test compound concentration at 0.1% vol/vol  DMSO. One day before infection (Day -1), test plates were equilibrated to room temperature and 30 µL of Vero E6 EGFP cells were added at 8,000 cells/well. On the day of infection (Day 0), plates were transported to the CAPS-IT robotic system for the addition of virus (MOI = 0.001) using a liquid handler (EVO 100, Tecan) to a final volume of 60 µl, and incubated at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 4 days. Plates were then imaged on an Arrayscan XTI, (Thermofisher)
At day four post-infection, the GFP signal was captured using wide field fluorescence imaging by exciting at 485-20 nm and emitting with the BGRFRN filter set. A 5 X objective captured 80% of the well bottom area in the 384 plate. The optimal exposure time was determined based on fluorescence intensity and was set as 0.023 seconds. A 2x2 binning was used and autofocus plane count was reduced to increase image acquisition speed. An image analysis protocol was developed in-house by using the SpotDetector bioapplication (Cellomics, Thermofisher). After background reduction on the raw image files, a fixed fluorescent intensity threshold was determined for the identification of fluorescent cells. Afterwards, the total area (‘total amount of surface covered by fluorescent cells’) was calculated for the test compounds and control wells.
Test well results are normalized relative to the corresponding +/- Remdesivir controls giving % confluence as primary endpoint. As secondary output, the number of cells/well were determined and normalisation to the corresponding +/- Remdesivir controls results in % inhibition. Outliers are eliminated according to 3-sigma method and plate level statistical performance is assessed using the standard Z’ calculation.

assay_name: GPR35 antagonism
assay_description: Although it has been described as a G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) activated by either kynurenic acid, or by the chemokine CXCL17, GPR35 remains an 'orphan'. It is, however, an interesting potential therapeutic target in both inflammatory bowel diseases, where a strong association with an open reading frame SNP of GPR35 provides genetic support, and in pain, based in part on its expression by dorsal root ganglion cells. There are very marked differences in agonist pharmacology between the mouse, rat and human orthologues of GPR35. Moreover, the only two compound series described as antagonists of GPR35 are both highly selective for the human orthologue and show little or no affinity for the rodent forms of the receptor and neither of these series provided a clear starting point for a 'drug-like' molecule. Almost all efforts to identify ligands for GPR35 have taken advantage of strong agonist-mediated interactions between orthologues of GPR35 and beta-arrestin adapter proteins because, among the G protein family GPR35 is highly selective for Galpha13 which is unable to directly regulate levels of secondary messengers and its activation is challenging to measure in high-throughput assay formats. Stable cells lines were generated in which it has been expressed either human or mouse GPR35 linked in frame, via a BRET-sensor, to the C-terminal 28 amino acids of Galpha13. These so called 'SPASM' sensor expressing cells generate a high signal to background (Mackenzie et al (2019) FASEB J. 33(4):5005-5017) and excellent Z' in BRET-based assays of agonism. The initial objective of the project is to identify antagonist ligands that (a) either display high affinity at both human and mouse GPR35 or (b) separate high affinity series that block either human or mouse GPR35. It is aimed to select series suitable for medicinal chemistry optimisation that are suitable as tool compounds to study the effects of this receptor in both ex vivo and in vivo settings

assay_name: Single dose inhibition of SARS-CoV-2 3CL-Pro
assay_description:
The activity of 3-CLpro (GenBank : MN908947.3 Residues 3264-3569) was measured by a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), using the dual-labeled substrate, DABCYL-KTSAVLQ↓SGFRKM-EDANS (Bachem #4045664) containing a protease specific cleavage site after the Gln. In the intact peptide, EDANS fluorescence is quenched by the DABCYL group. Following enzymatic cleavage, generation of the fluorescent product was monitored (Ex/Em = 340/460 nm) (EnVision, PerkinElmer). In the primary screen, test compounds (stock at 10 mM in 100 % DMSO), positive (zinc pyrithione (medchemexpress, #HY-B0572) 10 mM in 100 % DMSO) and negative (100 % DMSO) controls, were transferred to 384-well assay microplates by acoustic dispensing (Echo, Labcyte). Plate locations were: test compounds at 20 μM final (columns 1 to 22); positive control zinc pyrithione at 10 μM final (column 23); and negative control 0.2 % v/v (column 24). 5 μl of SARS-CoV-2 3CL-Pro stock (120 nM) in assay buffer (20 mM Tris (pH 7.3), 100 mM NaCl, and 1mM EDTA) were added to compound plates and incubated for 60 min at 37 °C. After addition of 5 μl substrate (30 μM in assay buffer), the final concentrations were: 15 μM substrate; 60 nM SARS-CoV-2 3CL-Pro; 20 μM compound; and 0.2 % DMSO in a total volume of 10 μL/well. The fluorescence signal was then measured at 15 min and inhibition (%) calculated relative to controls (Envision, PerkinElmer). Results were normalized to the 100 % (positive control) and 0 % (negative control) inhibition. To flag possible optical interference effects, primary assay plates were also read 60 min after substrate addition, when the reaction was complete. 
Outliers are eliminated according to 3-sigma method and plate level statistical performance is assessed using the standard Z’ calculation.

assay_name: Dose-dependent inhibition of SARS-CoV-2 3CL-Pro
assay_description:
The activity of 3-CLpro (GenBank : MN908947.3 Residues 3264-3569) was measured by a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), using the dual-labeled substrate, DABCYL-KTSAVLQ↓SGFRKM-EDANS (Bachem #4045664) containing a protease specific cleavage site after the Gln. In the intact peptide, EDANS fluorescence is quenched by the DABCYL group. Following enzymatic cleavage, generation of the fluorescent product was monitored (Ex/Em = 340/460 nm) (EnVision, PerkinElmer). In the hit profiling screen, test compounds (11-concentrations, 2-fold dilution series, top at 10 mM stock concentration in 100 % DMSO), positive (zinc pyrithione (medchemexpress, #HY-B0572) 10 mM in 100 % DMSO) and negative (100 % DMSO) controls, were transferred to 384-well assay microplates by acoustic dispensing (Echo, Labcyte). Plate locations were: test compounds at 0.02 µM - 20 μM final (columns 1 to 22); positive control zinc pyrithione at 10 μM final (column 23); and negative control 0.2 % v/v (column 24). 5 μl of SARS-CoV-2 3CL-Pro stock (120 nM) in assay buffer (20 mM Tris (pH 7.3), 100 mM NaCl, and 1mM EDTA) were added to compound plates and incubated for 60 min at 37 °C. After addition of 5 μl substrate (30 μM in assay buffer), the final concentrations were: 15 μM substrate; 60 nM SARS-CoV-2 3CL-Pro; 0.02 µM - 20 μM compound; and 0.2 % DMSO in a total volume of 10 μL/well. The fluorescence signal was then measured at 15 min and inhibition (%) calculated relative to controls. Results were normalized to the 100 % (positive control) and 0 % (negative control) inhibition. 
Outliers are eliminated according to 3-sigma method and plate level statistical performance is assessed using the standard Z’ calculation.
Dose-Response curve fits are performed using KNIME HCS tools (https://github.com/knime-mpicbg/HCS-Tools/wiki). The workflow is published on GitHUB (https://github.com/Fraunhofer-ITMP/KNIME/tree/main/Dose_response_workflow_biochemical) and KNIME hub (https://hub.knime.com/fraunhoferitmp/spaces/Public/latest/Dose_Response_Biochemical/DRCfit_biochemical_ECBD~6NLZB5Jkgn6j5a6Y). In brief,  all data is fitted accordign to a four-parameter free fit model. IC50 values were not extrapolated. Compounds were classified as active when: 
- maximum (average % inhibion) >25 % and slope is >0 and IC50 > 5 µM or
- minimum (average % inhibion) >75 %

assay_name: Cytotoxicty on All Sil and All Sil R cell lines
assay_description: Identify small molecules that specifically target a cell line with a mutated SERCA protein, which makes the cell line resistant to Thapsigargin. The targeted phenotype is cytotoxicity of the compounds, measured using a resazurin-based assay for detecting intracellular NADH level. For investigating the specificity, a cell line with mutated SERCA protein and an non-mutated cell line was used for comparison.

assay_name: BRET Assay for Mitochondrial ER Contact sites modulators
assay_description: A stable cell line containing a Bioluminescence Resonance Energy Transfer (BRET)-based biosensor for the quantitative analysis of distances between ER that allows to study contact site reversibility and dynamics. This BRET-based biosensor, consists in a mitochondrial outer membrane (MOM)-targeted Rluc8 (acting as donor) and ER-targeted mVenus (as acceptor), that allows studying the distance between mitochondria and the ER membrane with minimal interference.

assay_name: Identification of specific binders of calcineurin B homologous proteins (CHPs)
assay_description:
Identification of molecules that bind calcineurin B homologous proteins (CHPs) was undertaken using a fluorescent probe hydrophobicity assay.  ProteOrange (PO) is a fluorescent dye that has strong fluorescence in a hydrophobic environment and weak fluorescence when free in aqueous solutions. PO fluoresces strongly (λex/λem 485/590 nm) in the presence of CHP1,  which contains a hydrophobic pocket that binds NHE1. Compounds that modulate CHP1 activity would cause conformational changes to its hydrophobic pocket which could be observed by a reduction in PO fluorescence. 
Negative control is CHP1 with PO, positive control contains additional 10 uM of CMZ, a previously identified active compound. CMZ did not show significant fluorescence reduction in the primary screen. All compounds were screened at 10 uM.

assay_name: Counter screen for non-specific modulators of calcineurin B homologous proteins (CHPs)
assay_description:
To identify compounds that interfere with the fluorescence probe hydrophobicity method and are not specific to CHP1, compounds are tested in an assay where CHP1 has been substituted with BSA. PO shows increased fluorescence in the presence of BSA. Any compound that reduces this fluorescence is not specific to CHP1 and is assay interfering.
Negative control is BSA with PO, positive control contains free PO.
CMZ was tested at 10 uM and compounds identified as active in the primary screen were rescreened as triplicates at 10 uM.

assay_name: Dose response of specific binders of calcineurin B homologous proteins (CHPs)
assay_description:
Identification of molecules that bind calcineurin B homologous proteins (CHPs) was undertaken using a fluorescent probe hydrophobicity assay.  ProteOrange (PO) is a fluorescent dye that has strong fluorescence in a hydrophobic environment and weak fluorescence when free in aqueous solutions. PO fluoresces strongly (λex/λem 485/590 nm) in the presence of CHP1, which contains a hydrophobic pocket that binds NHE1. Compounds that modulate CHP1 activity would cause conformational changes to its hydrophobic pocket which could be observed by a reduction in PO fluorescence. 
Negative control is CHP1 with PO. Because CMZ did not show significant fluorescence reduction in the primary screen, positive control is free PO. Active compounds from the primary screen that did not interfere in the counter screen were tested at doses ranging from 0.1 to 25 uM and compared to CMZ at equivalent doses.

assay_name: Academic compound collection, Validation screening (EUACC Batch 06) cell viability ATP quantification assay with HepG2 cells
assay_description:
Validation screen with 24 compounds that showed at least 30% Inhibition in the primary screen. 
Plate type: 384-well plate Corning 3764 Black clear bottom.
750 cells /well seeded into the plate. 
Cell culture medium: DMEM (1.0 g/L glucose, without L-Glutamine, Lonza 12-707F), 10% FBS (Gibco 10270106), 2 mM L-glutamine (Lonza BE17-605E), 2% Pen/Strep (Lonza DE17-602E), + 1% NEAA (Gibco 11140-035)
 48 hour incubation time.
Cell viability ATP quantification assay with HepG2 cells. Assay kit: CellTiter-Glo®️ 2.0 Cell Viability Assay - Promega Corporation. 
15uL CellTiter-Glo/well added.
Compounds were tested with 8 doses in triplicates in independent experiments. Screening concentrations were 0.01, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 50 uM. Luminescence was detected using Pherastar FS reader. Data was normalized to the low (10 uM Sepantronium bromide - YM155) and high controls (25 nL/well Dimethyl Sulfoxide - DMSO) to generate percent inhibition values. The percent inhibition values uploaded. 
 Curve fitting was done using four parameter hill fitting algorithm. AUC, IC50, low and high asymptote values are reported.

assay_name: C. auris DSM21092 Anti-Fungal Assay
assay_description:
Screening of the EU-OS compound library (101.024 compounds) for 1 concentration (50 μM) with 2 replicates against C. auris DSM21092, according to “N8: Anti-Bacterial and Anti-Fungal Assays Bioprofiling Handbook” and following the protocols optimized during the validation step indicated in that proposal (Under the frame of the EU-OS bioprofiling tender process), to determine the antifungal properties of these compounds against this human pathogen.
Briefly, compounds were tested in a liquid growth medium dispensed in 384 well plates inoculated with a standardized fungal suspension (CFU/mL) and after overnight incubation at 37ºC the plates were examined for fungal growth as absorbance readout. The Genedata Screener software (Genedata, Inc., Basel, Switzerland) was used to process and analyze all the data from the screen. The reproducibility and sensitivity were supported by the statistical values derived from all the experiments performed. The activity of the compounds was expressed as a percentage of growth inhibition where 100% represented the inhibition growth of the target microorganism and 0% represented the total growth of target microorganism on the assay.

assay_name: Inhibition of MeCP2/TBL1 binding
assay_description: Identification of small molecules that inhibit MeCP2 binding to TBL1/TBLR1 as a potential therapy for MeCP2 duplication syndrome. Binding is measured by a reduction in NanoBRET, where the two NanoLuc subunits SmBiT and LgBiT are fused to MeCP2 and TBL1, respectively. Interruption of the binding interaction of MeCP2/TBL1 results in dissociation of the functional luciferase and thus reduction in luminescence in the presence of furimazine substrate.

assay_name: Counterscreen for MeCP2/TBL1 binding assay interference
assay_description: Counter screen to identify molecules that non-specifically inhibit the NanoBRET assay of MeCP2/TBL1, using complementation of cAMP-dependent protein kinase. The regulatory subunit of Protein kinase A is independently labelled with SmBiT and LgBiT which constitute an active luciferase when PKA homodimerizes. The luciferase generates a luminescence signal in the presence of substrate. Any compounds that reduce the luminescence in this assay are non-specific inhibitors of the MeCP2/TBL1 interaction and likely NanoBRET interfering compounds. Hits from the primary screen were tested in triplicate at 20 uM.

assay_name: Dose response inhibition of MeCP2/TBL1 binding
assay_description: Validation of small molecules identified as inhibitors of MeCP2 binding to TBL1/TBLR1 from a fixed-dose primary screen in a dose response assay using doses ranging from 0.625 to 50 uM. Binding is measured by a reduction in NanoBRET, where the two NanoLuc subunits SmBiT and LgBiT are fused to MeCP2 and TBL1, respectively. Interruption of the binding interaction of MeCP2/TBL1 results in dissociation of the functional luciferase and thus reduction in luminescence in the presence of furimazine substrate.

assay_name: C. albicans ATCC 64124 Anti-Fungal Assay
assay_description:
Screening of 523 compounds from the EU-OS compound library (101.024 compounds) for Hit Confirmation in Dose Response Curves with 3 replicates against C. albicans ATCC64124, according to “N8: Anti-Bacterial and Anti-Fungal Assays Bioprofiling Handbook” and following the protocols optimized during the validation step indicated in that proposal (Under the frame of the EU-OS bioprofiling tender process), to determine the antibacterial properties of these compounds against this human pathogen.
Briefly, compounds were tested in a liquid growth medium dispensed in 384 well plates inoculated with a standardized fungal suspension (CFU/mL) and after overnight incubation at 37ºC the plates were examined for bacterial growth as absorbance readout. Each compound was serially diluted in DMSO to provide 7 different final assay concentrations: 50 µM, 10 µM, 3 µM, 1 µM, 0.3 µM, 0.1 µM and 0.01 µM. The Genedata Screener software (Genedata, Inc., Basel, Switzerland) was used to process and analyze all the data from the screen. The reproducibility and sensitivity were supported by the statistical values derived from all the experiments performed. The activity of the compounds was expressed as a percentage of growth inhibition where 100% represented the inhibition growth of the target microorganism and 0% represented the total growth of target microorganism on the assay.

assay_name: KCNQ3 FluxOR(TM) Potassium Ion Channel Assay - Primary Screen
assay_description:
The assay monitores the uptake of thallium via KCNQ3 potassium channel using the FluxOR(TM) thallium indicator dye.

Detailed Procedure:
1.	Cell seeding: 4.000 cells (CHO-KCNQ3 A315T) per 384 well in 25 µl,
2.	Removal of culture medium: 24 h after seeding the growth medium was aspirated completely (manually,)
3.	Addition of loading buffer: 15 µl of loading buffer plus backdrop reagent (see below), cells were loaded for 30-60 min
4.	Addition of compounds, positive (10 µM Retigarbine, col 23) and negative controls (DMSO, col 24): cmpds from labcyte plates were transferred using the labcyte echo (15 nl per well)
5.	Pre-stimulus scan: 2 scans were acquired using the FI bottom read protocol (Exc: 460 - 490 nm, Em: 520 - 540 nm)
6.	Addition of stimulus buffer: 5 µl of stimulus buffer per well by a 16 multichannel pipet
7.	Kinetic reading: Revvity Envision reader, for kinetic reading multiple scans were recorded up to 5 min (45 sec per 384 well plate scan) using FI bottom read protocol (Exc: 460 - 490 nm, Em: 520 - 540 nm)

1x Loading buffer (total volume of 10 ml per plate): 
100 µl Power load Concentrate (100x, Component D), 10 µl FluxOR II Reagent (dissolved in DMSO), 7,8 ml A.dest, 1 ml FluxOR Assay Buffer (10x, Component C), 100 µl Probenicid (from Kit), 1 ml Backdrop Suppressor Reagent for Microplate Assays (BackDrop™, B10512)

Stimulus buffer (total volume of 10 ml per plate):
2 mL Chloride-free Stimulus Buffer, 1 mL Thallium Sulfate (50 mM stock from Kit), 7 ml A.dest 

Analysis:
Normalisation of the Fluorescence intensity to the pre-stimulus intensity:  Intensity = RFU(t=50s)/RFU(t=0s)
Activity Normalisation based on positive (Retigarbine, 100%) and negative (DMSO, 0%) control.
Outliers are eliminated according to 3-sigma method and plate level statistical performance is assessed using the standard Z’ calculation.
Selected as hits were a total of 48 compounds, namely 1% of screened compounds with highest activity were classified "Agonist",  1% of screened compounds with lowest (negative) activity were classified "Inhibitor" and were selected for hit confirmation.
Analyses were Performed using Activity Base (IDBS), Knime (Knime AG), and Spotfire (PerkinElmer)

assay_name: Academic compound collection, Validation screening (EUACC Batch 03) cell viability ATP quantification assay with HepG2 cells
assay_description:
Validation screen with 207 compounds that showed at least 30% Inhibition in the primary screen. 
Plate type: 384-well plate Corning 3764 Black clear bottom.
750 cells /well seeded into the plate. 
Cell culture medium: DMEM (1.0 g/L glucose, without L-Glutamine, Lonza 12-707F), 10% FBS (Gibco 10270106), 2 mM L-glutamine (Lonza BE17-605E), 2% Pen/Strep (Lonza DE17-602E), + 1% NEAA (Gibco 11140-035)
 48 hour incubation time.
Cell viability ATP quantification assay with HepG2 cells. Assay kit: CellTiter-Glo®️ 2.0 Cell Viability Assay - Promega Corporation. 
15uL CellTiter-Glo/well added.
Compounds were tested with 8 doses in triplicates in independent experiments. Screening concentrations were 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 50 uM. Luminescence was detected using Pherastar FS reader. Data was normalized to the low (10 uM Sepantronium bromide - YM155) and high controls (25 nL/well Dimethyl Sulfoxide - DMSO) to generate percent inhibition values. The percent inhibition values uploaded. 
 Curve fitting was done using four parameter hill fitting algorithm. AUC, IC50, low and high asymptote values are reported.

assay_name: KCNQ3 FluxOR(TM) Potassium Ion Channel Assay - hit profiling
assay_description:
The assay monitores the uptake of thallium via KCNQ3 potassium channel using the FluxOR(TM) thallium indicator dye.

Detailed Procedure:
1.	Cell seeding: 4.000 cells (CHO-KCNQ3 A315T) per 384 well in 25 µl,
2.	Removal of culture medium: 24 h after seeding the growth medium was aspirated completely (manually,)
3.	Addition of loading buffer: 15 µl of loading buffer plus backdrop reagent (see below), cells were loaded for 30-60 min
4.	Addition of compounds, positive (10 µM Retigarbine, col 23) and negative controls (DMSO, col 24): cmpds from labcyte plates were transferred using the labcyte echo (15 nl per well)
5.	Pre-stimulus scan: 2 scans were acquired using the FI bottom read protocol (Exc: 460 - 490 nm, Em: 520 - 540 nm)
6.	Addition of stimulus buffer: 5 µl of stimulus buffer per well by a 16 multichannel pipet
7.	Kinetic reading: Revvity Envision reader, for kinetic reading multiple scans were recorded up to 5 min (45 sec per 384 well plate scan) using FI bottom read protocol (Exc: 460 - 490 nm, Em: 520 - 540 nm)

1x Loading buffer (total volume of 10 ml per plate): 
100 µl Power load Concentrate (100x, Component D), 10 µl FluxOR II Reagent (dissolved in DMSO), 7,8 ml A.dest, 1 ml FluxOR Assay Buffer (10x, Component C), 100 µl Probenicid (from Kit), 1 ml Backdrop Suppressor Reagent for Microplate Assays (BackDrop™, B10512)

Stimulus buffer (total volume of 10 ml per plate):
2 mL Chloride-free Stimulus Buffer, 1 mL Thallium Sulfate (50 mM stock from Kit), 7 ml A.dest 

Analysis:
Normalisation of the fluorescence intensity to the pre-stimulus intensity:  Intensity = RFU(t=50s)/RFU(t=0s)
Activity Normalisation based on positive (Retigarbine, 100%) and negative (DMSO, 0%) control.
Outliers are eliminated according to 3-sigma method and plate level statistical performance is assessed using the standard Z’ calculation. Analyses of Dose-responses were performed using 4-parameter logisitc curve fit. Analyses were performed using Activity Base (IDBS), Knime (Knime AG),  Spotfire (Revvity), and Prism (GraphPad)

assay_name: E. faecalis ATCC 29212 Anti-Bacterial Assay
assay_description:
Screening of 523 compounds from the EU-OS compound library (101.024 compounds) for Hit Confirmation in Dose Response Curves with 3 replicates against E. faecalis ATCC 29212, according to “N8: Anti-Bacterial and Anti-Fungal Assays Bioprofiling Handbook” and following the protocols optimized during the validation step indicated in that proposal (Under the frame of the EU-OS bioprofiling tender process), to determine the antibacterial properties of these compounds against this human pathogen.
Briefly, compounds were tested in a liquid growth medium dispensed in 384 well plates inoculated with a standardized bacterial suspension (CFU/mL) and after overnight incubation at 37ºC the plates were examined for bacterial growth as absorbance readout. Each compound was serially diluted in DMSO to provide 7 different final assay concentrations: 50 µM, 10 µM, 3 µM, 1 µM, 0.3 µM, 0.1 µM and 0.01 µM. The Genedata Screener software (Genedata, Inc., Basel, Switzerland) was used to process and analyze all the data from the screen. The reproducibility and sensitivity were supported by the statistical values derived from all the experiments performed. The activity of the compounds was expressed as a percentage of growth inhibition where 100% represented the inhibition growth of the target microorganism and 0% represented the total growth of target microorganism on the assay.

assay_name: Solubility
assay_description: Solubility of compounds is a key issue for carrying out high throughput screening campaigns, since it will condition the highest concentration to be employed in the assays, as well as the percentage of DMSO to be added to guarantee that the compound does not either precipitate or aggregate in the assay. For knowing the solubility of compounds it was carried out kinetic solubility analysis of EU-OPENSCREEN chemical library thorugh nepehlometric measurements. The screening was carried out at 10 µM with duplicate points for each compound tested. Nephelometry values were corrected and those compounds showing values lower than 50000 units were classified as high soluble compounds, those with values in the range between 50000 and 100000 untis as medium soluble compounds and those with values higher than 100000 units as low soluble compounds. The compounds with medium and low solubility were re-evaluated at three concentrations for a better classification.

assay_name: Determination of compound Redox activity using conversion of resazurin to resorufin with DTT
assay_description:
The resazurin - DTT based assay was performed on 851 previously selected compounds from ECBL screening library in 8 concentrations (concentration range: 0.0125 – 50 µM) in duplicates (duplicate plates). All compounds were in 10 mM stocks (some of them were also ordered in less concentrated stocks) on LDV 384-well plate (384 LDV Labcyte # LP-0200). A 100-fold diluted compound plates were prepared by transfer 80 nL using Echo 650T (Labcyte) acoustic liquid handler to 384-well plates (384 LDV Labcyte # LP-0200) and diluted by dispense 8 µL DMSO (VWR # 83673.290) using a non-contact dispenser Multidrop Combi (ThermoFisher). Source plates and prepared plates with 100 times diluted stock solutions were thawed and centrifuged prior to transferring of 5; 15; 50 nL (from 10 mM stocks) and 2.5; 5; 15; 50 and 157.5 nL (from 0.1 mM stocks) to the assay plates (384-well black low volume plates, Greiner #784076) using Echo 650T (Labcyte) acoustic liquid handler. DMSO was also transferred by ECHO to a volume of 157.5 nL. DMSO and NSC 663284 (Tocris # 1867) were added as negative and positive controls to the columns 23 and 24 of each assay plate to yield 1.6% and 1,5 µM final concentration respectively. Then, Multidrop Combi (ThermoFisher) non-contact dispenser was used to add 10 µL solution of 5 µM resazurin (Pol-Aura # PA-03-5198) in 50 mM HEPES buffer (Sigma # H3375) with 50 mM sodium chloride, pH 7.5 containing either 50 µM DTT (Bioshop Canada # DTT001) per well. Plates were incubated for 60 min at RT and fluorescent signal was detected at Ex = 560 ± 8 nm/Em = 590 ± 8 nm using ClarioStar (BMG Labtech) plate reader.
KNIME pipeline was generated and applied for analysis of the results. The raw data files (one txt file per plate) were read and matched with plate template followed by calculation of averages and standard deviation of controls and compound wells, and Z’ factor values for each plate. The plates were proceeded in the loop, generating one data set for all tested plates. Automatic outliers’ removal was used for detecting control outliers for plates with Z’ factor values below 0,5.  The plates for which removal of the outliers did not lead to improvement of Z’ factor (to value higher than 0,5) or the algorithm was detecting more than 2 outliers, were retested. 
The raw data values were normalized to positive and negative controls for each plate, to calculate percent activation following the formula: % ACTIVATION = ((x- μn)/ μp – μn))*100, where X represents readout VALUE of the well,  μn represents the mean of the negative controls and μp the mean of the positive controls for each tested assay plate. The heat maps of % ACTIVATION were then generated for each plate in order to identify plate effects or signal distribution patterns.  Plates with visible patterns or abnormal distribution were retested.
From the data table, the compound's concentration, % activation, and compound ID were selected and using a pipeline in KNIME and a node that utilizes R programming, dose-response curves were generated for each compound. All curves were generated using LL.4 regression model. The EC50 values were determined from the inflection points of the generated curves. 
In relation to the conducted assay aimed to identify compounds capable of generating ROS, which may cause interferences in other assays, the following criterion was adopted. All compounds were marked as ACTIVE when they exhibited activity above 30% in two concentrations and two replicates. All active compounds that exhibited an assay response (above 30%) inversely proportional to the tested concentration were labeled as INCONCLUSIVE. The determined EC50 values for compounds with insufficient fitting of dose-response curves or in the case of observing bell-shaped dose-response were omitted. Other additional information was placed in the comments in results file.

All curves were generated using LL.4 regression model. The EC50 values were determined from the inflection points of the generated curves. 
In relation to the conducted assay aimed to identify compounds capable of generating ROS, which may cause interferences in other assays, the following criterion was adopted. All compounds were marked as ACTIVE when they exhibited activity above 30% in two concentrations and two replicates.

assay_name: Determination of compound Redox activity using conversion of resazurin to resorufin with DTT
assay_description:
To identify Redox Oxidative Species,  the resazurin based assay was performed on the whole ECBL screening library at single concentration of 10 µM in duplicates (duplicate plates). The compound plates (384 LDV Labcyte # LP-0200) were thawed and centrifuged prior to transferring of 10 nL of 10 mM stocks to the assay plates (384-well black low volume plates, Greiner #784076) using Echo 650T (Labcyte) acoustic liquid handler. DMSO (VWR # 83673.290) and NSC 663284 (Tocris # 1867) were added as negative and positive controls to the columns 23 and 24 of each assay plate to yield 0.1% and 1,5 µM final concentration respectively. Then, Multidrop Combi (ThermoFisher) non-contact dispenser was used to add 10 µL solution of 5 µM resazurin (Pol-Aura # PA-03-5198) in 50 mM Hepes buffer (Sigma # H3375) with 50 mM sodium chloride, pH 7.5 containing 50 µM DTT (Bioshop Canada # DTT001) per well. Plates were incubated for 60 min at RT and fluorescent signal was detected at Ex = 560 ± 8 nm/Em = 590 ± 8 nm using ClarioStar (BMG Labtech) plate reader.
KNIME pipeline was generated and applied for analysis of the results. The raw data files (one txt file per plate) were read and matched with plate template followed by calculation of averages and standard deviation of controls and compound wells, and Z’ factor values for each plate. The plates were proceeded in the loop, generating one data set for all tested plates. Automatic outliers removal was used for detecting control outliers for plates with Z’ factor values below 0,5.  The plates for which removal of the outliers did not lead to improvement of Z’ factor (to value higher than 0,5) or the algorithm was detecting more than 2 outliers, were retested. 
The raw data values were normalised to positive and negative controls for each plate, to calculate percent activation following the formula: % ACTIVATION = ((x- μn)/ μp – μn))*100, where X represents Relative Fluorescence Units (RFU) detected for the well,  μn the mean of the negative controls and μp the mean of the positive controls for each tested assay plate. In addition, Z-score values were calculated for each plate according to the formula: Z score= (x – μ) / σ, where X represents RFU detected for the well, μ the mean and σ standard deviation of RFU for all compounds on the plate.
To identify active compounds the thresholds of 30% ACTIVATION was applied to % ACTIVATION data as well Z-score above 3 to Z-score values. Compounds showing activity above the thresholds for both duplicates were flagged as ACTIVES and compounds with activity above the threshold for only one replicate were re-tested in triplicates. After re-testing, compounds with at least two replicates above the thresholds were flagged as ACTIVES.

The plates were proceeded in the loop, generating one data set for all tested plates. Automatic outliers removal was used for detecting control outliers for plates with Z’ factor values below 0,5.  The plates for which removal of the outliers did not lead to improvement of Z’ factor (to value higher than 0,5) or the algorithm was detecting more than 2 outliers, were retested. 
The raw data values were normalised to positive and negative controls for each plate, to calculate percent activation following the formula: % ACTIVATION = ((x- μn)/ μp – μn))*100, where X represents Relative Fluorescence Units (RFU) detected for the well,  μn the mean of the negative controls and μp the mean of the positive controls for each tested assay plate. In addition, Z-score values were calculated for each plate according to the formula: Z score= (x – μ) / σ, where X represents RFU detected for the well, μ the mean and σ standard deviation of RFU for all compounds on the plate.
To identify active compounds the thresholds of 30% ACTIVATION was applied to % ACTIVATION data as well Z-score above 3 to Z-score values. Compounds showing activity above the thresholds for both duplicates were flagged as ACTIVES and compounds with activity above the threshold for only one replicate were re-tested in triplicates. After re-testing, compounds with at least two replicates above the thresholds were flagged as ACTIVES.

assay_description:

Plates were incubated with compounds for 30 min at RT and then 10 uL of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) (Sigma #77332) solution was added to yield final concentration of 60ug/ml with phenol red (Pol-Aura #212722504) (final concentration100ug/ml) in HBSS using Multidrop Combi with small cassette. After 30 min incubation at RT, the reaction was terminated by addition of 10 uL of 1 M NaOH (POCH # 810981424) with Multidrop Combi. In addition, Z-score values were calculated for each plate according to the formula: Z score= (x – μ) / σ, where X represents Absorbance values detected for the well, μ the mean and σ standard deviation of Absorbance values for all compounds on the plate.

assay_name: Inhibition of NanoLuc Luciferase activity
assay_description:
To identify compounds interfering with Nano-Luc luciferase in ECBL screening library an enzymatic assay was applied.  The compound plates (384 LDV Labcyte # LP-0200) containing 10 mM compound solutions in DMSO  were thawed and centrifuged prior to transferring of 20  nL of 10 mM stocks to the assay plates (LUMITRAC 384 white, Greiner #781075) using Echo 650T (Labcyte) acoustic liquid handler. DMSO (VWR # 83673.290) and  Isradipine (Tocris #2004) were added as negative and positive controls to the columns 23 and 24 of each assay plate to yield 0.1% and 10 µM final concentration respectively. Multidrop Combi (ThermoFisher) non-contact dispenser was used to add 10 µL solution of 20 pM NanoLuc enzyme (Promega # E499A) in PBS (ThermoFisherScientific # 10010-015) with 0,1% BSA (MP Biomedicals # 160069) per well and compounds were pre-incubated with the enzyme for 10 min at RT.  Then, the enzymatic reaction was initiated by adding of 10 µL furimazine (Aobious # AOB36539) solution in PBS with 0,1% BSA to final concentration of 10 µM (final Nano-Luc concentration 10 pM, final compound concentration 10 µM) with Multidrop Combi. After 10 min luminescent signal was measured with the integration time of 0,2 sec using ClarioStar (BMG Labtech) plate reader.
KNIME pipeline was used for analysis of the results. The raw data files (one txt file per plate) were read and matched with plate template followed by calculation of averages and standard deviation of controls and compound wells, and Z’ factor values for each plate. The plates were proceeded in the loop, generating one data set for all tested plates. Automatic outliers removal was used for detecting control outliers for plates with Z’ factor values below 0,5.  The plates for which removal of the outliers did not lead to improvement of Z’ factor (to value higher than 0,5) or the algorithm was detecting more than 2 outliers, were retested. 
The raw data values were normalised to positive and negative controls for each plate, to calculate percent inhibition following the formula: % INHIBITION = (1-(x- μp)/ μn – μp))*100, where X represents Relative Luminescence Units (RLU) detected for the well,  μn the mean of the negative controls and μp the mean of the positive controls for each tested assay plate. To identify active compounds the thresholds of 30% was applied. Compounds showing %INHIBITION above 30% and below -30% were consider as hits- inhibitors and activators respectively. All the primary hits were then re-tested in duplicates under the same assay conditions. Compounds for which the activity was confirmed for at least one replicate (above 30 or below -30 %INHIBITION) are flagged as ACTIVE.

assay_name: Inhibition of ARC4/tankyrase binding motif interaction
assay_description:
The assay is based on the interaction of the human TNKS2 protein binding domain ARC4 with a tankyrase binding motif consisting of 8 amino acids. The ARC4 domain is produced as fusion protein with cyan fluorescent protein (CFP, variant mCerulean), while the tankyrase binding motif is produced as fusion protein with yellow fluorescent protein (YFP, variant mCitrine). Proteins are recombinantly produced in E. coli in Oulu.
Upon interaction, the CFP is brought into proximity of the YFP, resulting in a Förster-resonance-energy transfer (FRET) from excitation of CFP at 430 nm resulting in emission from YFP at 527 nm.  We therefore measure the fluorescence intensity of the emission of CFP (477 nm, donor emission) and YFP (527 nm, acceptor emission) upon CFP excitation at 430 nm. We calculate the ratiometric FRET signal (YFP emission:CFP emission, r430) after fluorescence readout and blank subtraction for each well.

assay_name: MSSA ATCC 29213 Anti-Bacterial Assay
assay_description:
Screening of 46 compounds from the EACL Academic Compound library (Batch1+Batch2) for Hit Confirmation in Dose Response Curves with 3 replicates against MSSA ATCC 29213, according to “N8: Anti-Bacterial and Anti-Fungal Assays Bioprofiling Handbook” and following the protocols optimized during the validation step indicated in that proposal (Under the frame of the EU-OS bioprofiling tender process), to determine the antibacterial properties of these compounds against this human pathogen.
Briefly, compounds were tested in a liquid growth medium dispensed in 384 well plates inoculated with a standardized bacterial suspension (CFU/mL) and after overnight incubation at 37ºC the plates were examined for bacterial growth as absorbance readout. Each compound was serially diluted in DMSO to provide 7 different final assay concentrations: 50 µM, 10 µM, 3 µM, 1 µM, 0.3 µM, 0.1 µM and 0.01 µM. The Genedata Screener software (Genedata, Inc., Basel, Switzerland) was used to process and analyze all the data from the screen. The reproducibility and sensitivity were supported by the statistical values derived from all the experiments performed. The activity of the compounds was expressed as a percentage of growth inhibition where 100% represented the inhibition growth of the target microorganism and 0% represented the total growth of target microorganism on the assay.
